: CURRENT KFFAIRS,

An influential correspondent, in the course of a letter
to us, describes the situation which the recent resolution
of a majority at last meeting of the Gaelic League Exc-
cutive has created, in this way :—'‘To me the position at
present seems to be this—A. attacks B in the street, in
the interest—as he states there and then and subse-
quently in the Independent—of © (whose badge he
wears—but this is not material). Now, unless C is
privy to or  approves of .A.’s mlgconducc
1 he  should immediately, on its coming to

 his knowledge, repudiate and condemn it in the
strongest terms, otherwise the general public can only
at O instigated A’s action, or is highly delighted
or else that C is in mortal dread of A.”’ ®

Central Executive adds a gross insult to a deep injury.
I attended in my official capacity as a member of the
Corporation. My horses were seized after the fashion
of highwaymen ; I was thrust out of the Procession, and
the coachman was threatened with a score of weapons.
I was warned that blood would be spilt if T attempted
to move on, and the only excuse offered me for this
monstrous outrage is that I had given a seat to the
Editor of the Leaper. I am aware that no personal
objection was held to me, but the persons present in my
carriage were my guests just as if they were in my house.
If T happened to represent the Corporation of Dublin in
the least civilised city in the world, in Bucharest or
Belgrade, in Constantinople or Cadiz, and if such an in-
cident occurred, I should see that ample reparation was
given in an hour’s time. Nor can I believe that anyone
in authority would dare to suggest that they ought not
to apologise for the affront because they had not officially
ordered it. The position of the majority of the Execu-
tive of the Gaelic League, as I understand it, now is
that any steward may with impunity insult any repre-
sentative person invited to the Procession, may even
commit a criminal assault on him, and that they need
not even apologise unless they have actually ordered the
incident! Sir, I greatly fear that the public will be
slow to trust their moneys to men whose views of re-
sponsibility are so peculiar.—Yours truly, J. C.
M‘WALTER.”’

A letter in similar, though not identical, terms also
appeared over Dr. M‘Walter’s signature in the Indepen-
dent of the previous day.

In the course of a note headed “ Imsulting a Jour-
nalist,”” in last week’s issue of the Cork Sun, our con-
temporary having quoted from Dr. McWalter’s
letter, adds:— Monstrous, abominable outrage, it was
disgraceful to all vesponsible for it, and the Executive
of the Gaclic League are blameworthy in refusing to
insist on an apology being made. It is safe to say that
the editor of the Lzaper has done a thousand times
more for the language cause than a single one of the
ignorant spalpini who assailed him and his host in the
procession. 1t says little for the wisdom, tact, or in-
telligence of the heads of the organisation that they
should, by their silence, allow such an outrage to go
unrebuked.’’

One day last weel the Annual General Meeting of
the County Wexford Gaclic League was held. There
was a very large amount of business gone through, for
the movement . has taken a great hold in Wexford

THE

County ‘and the movement there is self-supporting and
pays its own organiser and teachers. A great interest
« is taken in the industrial revival, and the industrial ex-
hibition which will be held at the Wexford Feis at
New Ross promises to be a great success.  Father
Harpur proposed a resolution calling upon public bodies
to support Irish manufacture whether dearer or not
than competing goods.  We read in a local report,
“That, he said, was a vo rong proposition, and
opposed to THE LEADER policy Well, it certainly is;
and Irish goods at any price is certainly a “strong” pro-
position. If Father Harpur who, we believe is young
and enthusiastic, if not exactly shrewd and level-
‘headed—these qualities will no doubt come with ex-
perience—if Father Harpur will read the letters from
leading manufacturers in last week’s LEADER, and in
the current issue, he will find—we feel sure such a
sweeping enthusiast will rémain loyally unconvinced—
that his “strong ’’ proposition is not only opposed to
the LeapEr policy, but opposed to the views of the
manufacturers whose views are set forth in the LEADER.
The “strong’’ proposition was, of course, objected to,
and the result was that the following resolution was
adopted : — That we call upon all the public boards in
this country to make contracts only with bona fide Irish
firms, and use only Irish manufactured goods except
where reasonable necessity compels them to do other-
wise.”” Certainly “ reasonable necessity '’ is an emi-
nently diplomatic qualification, and every one can in-
terpret it at his own sweet will.

The Leaper incident, or rather the fact that the
majority of the Executive Committee at the last meet-
ing refused to denounce the criminal conduct of street
blackguards came on for discussion. We quote from a
local press report :— Father Kehoe moved a resolution
received from one of the county branches of the league
expressing disappointment at the attitude of the central
branch of the league in not repudiating the disgraceful
treatment meted out to the editor of THE LEADER at the
recent language procession held in Dublin. An appeal
had been made to the Gaelic League authorities in
Dublin, said Father Kehoe, to repudiate all connection
with TEE LEADER incident, but they had refused to
do so. He thought that this meeting should repudiate
the action of the individuals in Dublin for the dis-
graceful manner in which they conducted them-
selves towards the Editor of the LEADER—a man
who had done so much for the advancement of
the Gaelic League. He thought it was a scandal and
a shame that a man like Mr. Moran should be treated
in such a disgraceful and undignified manner on such
an occasion in Dublin—a man, he said, he liked better
for the support that he had given the Gaelic movement
than any of them.”

We read that Father Harpur, whose “strong’’ in-
dustrial proposition was opposed to the LEADER policy,
“argued contra.”” He held that the promoters of the
procession were entitled to act as they liked with regard
to that procession, and allow into it or put out of it
whom they choose.  Father Harpur in his enthusiasm
cvidently forgot that even the Executive declared that
the street blackguards acted without authority! And
“ subsequently when Father McCormack remarked that
one of the roughs wore a badge, Father Harpur who had
previously objected to Father Keogh’s resolution on the
ground that the authorities of the procession could
5 allow in or put out whom they wished, shifted his
- ground and replied that “it was explained that the
 person in question wore a badge held over from last
year’s procession.””  Who told Father Harpur that?

as it been officially stated Did one of the street
‘blackguards who laid contribution on the services of a
~ “minion ”’ of the law in the shape of a policeman,
‘masquerade as a steward with a bogus badge, and yet
“are the authorities of the Leaguo afraid to denounce

h street scoundrels? However, the question whether
he badge was last year’s or this year’s is not very im-
ortant ; the fellow had a badge anyway, and he and his
treet roughs have not been denounced by a

LEADER. Aprin 29, 1905.

ajority ab the last meeting of the Exccutive Com-
::t]::)clcﬂy We quote further from the local press repor'(;;1
— ¢« Father Kohoe deprecated the action of the official
organ of the Gaelic League n not repudiating the in-
cident and standing up more strenuously for the prin-
ciples of the Gaelic League.””  Further on Father
Kohoe, as reported, said:—* Where, he asked, ;von;gl{d
the Gaclic League be to-day but for TuE LEADER e
would not be satisfied with a mere formal expression of
rogret roegarding a line of conduct that §1mu1d b‘i
vigorously condemned.’”’ Eventually at this annual
general meeting of the County Wexford (}n.ellc League,
held on the Monday after the Executive in Dublin had
passed a resolution refusing to denounce street black-
guardism, the following resolution was adopted on the
proposition of Father Kehoe, seconded by' TFather
MecCormack :— That we consider that anything that
would lessen the influence or the interests of THE LEADER
would be injurious to the best interests of TIrish Ireland,
and that we condemn the conduct of those persons who
were responsible for the treatment to the editor of that
journal.”

This resolution, as far as the County Wexford is con-
cerned, shows that there are  true friends of the move-
ment ”’ anxious to disassociate a noble and honourable
cause from all connection with criminal street black-
guardism and foot-pad tactics. Will the Executive
Committee that refused to denounce criminal street
blackguardism committed in the name of the League,
and who called upon “all true friends of the movement
to avoid further controversy in the matter,’”” now pro-
ceed to cut off the County Wexford from their manly
and self-respecting Executive? Surely Wexford, having
in defiance of the advice of the Executive denounced the
street blackguards—surely Wexford is now a qualified
“rotten limb’’ and should be amputated at once!

The following appeared in the official organ of the
Gaelic League, that it will be remembered neither ans-
wered our recent questions mor our criticisms :— This
decision  (the resolution of the Dublin Coisde
Ceanntair) had been appealed against by one or
two Craobhacha, and others had written to the Coisde
protesting against the appeal and urging the Coisde
Gnotha to endorse the resolution of the Coisde Ceann-
tair.”” 'We turn up our back numbers and we find that
the following branches took action concerning the crimi-
nal street blackguardism committed in the name of the
League that a majority of the Executive Committee has
refused to denounce:—Cavan Branch, the Keating
Branch, the Newcastle West Coipoe Ceanncaip, Lime-
rick Gaelic League, Clonmel Branch, Cleaver Branch,
Dublin ; Father Sherlock Branch, Birmingham. In our
issue of April 22nd we published resolutions from Water-
ford City Branch, Macroom, Queenstown, Cookstown
(Co. Tyrone), South Wexford District Committee. Well,
leaving out the latter five items and assuming that these
resolutions were not within the knowledge of the officials
at the time of the meeting, we find that there were at
least six branches and a Coipoe Ceanncaip. Yet in the
official  organ  these seven  protests  become
“one or two.”” “Others’’ approved the resolution of
the Dublin Corroe Ceanntaip we are told. If seven
equals “ one or two,”” what is  others *’ equal to? Let
us have the names of the “others ’—the sympathizers
with those who refused to condemn criminal street black-
g‘\mrd_ism ought not to have much modesty in them.
Turning seven into “ome or two’’ may appear for the
moment very clever to some people, but these sort of
crooked tactics happily defeat themselves. Public contro-
versies are not settled in the heat of an excited moment
in which those who show the most ingenuity in mawling

the number seven so that it would look like “one or
two’’ stand to win.

Now we faced the untrodden field nearly five years
ago. We hit out hard and fast, for we knew that it was
death to us if there was not defiance and resolution and
strength in every blow. We made frontal attacks on the

Of course, that is a situation that cannot

The League as a whole is all right, and

The Executive Committee has been selected up to this

What occurred at the E,xecutive meeting 1

As the

The olack-
guards laid contribution on a policeman to remove Dr.

• of the Executive at the last meeting declined to con-
demn. 
We suggest that the public are entitled to a

meeting and an account of the votes recorded.

Idolatrous policemen in the D.M.P. Force frequently
_feel what it is, from an economic point of view, not to
be “saved.”” Constables looking for promotion must pass
- a literary examination and drill and duty test. In both

cases an intending candidate makes application to his
_superintendent to be allowed to compete. The literary
examination was formerly held hall-yearly, but now
takes place annually. It is carried out by the Civil
_Service Commissioners.  No applicants are prevented
-from competing in this examination ; it is only a qualify-
~lug examination, and candidates pass it who receive one-
third marks in every subject. The results of these
examinations are sent to the Secretary’s Office at Dublin
“Castle, and from thence to the different Superintendents’
offices, and each candidate may receive information con-
cerning his marks on applying to his Superintendent.
It is somewhat different in the drill and duty test. For
some reason or another all applicants are not allowed to
“compete in this. An ‘‘ Idolatrous "’ superintendent and
_a “saved "’ superintendent are appointed examiners, and
an elderly lady well known in mission hall and “ birds’
“nests’’ circles is a frequent visitor at the Superintendents’
office. We wonder what would the Orange drum do if an
“elderly “ Idolatrous™ lady known to be keenly interested
“in the temporal success of “Idolators’ was taking up
the time of police officials, paid out of the public money,
with various interviews? A school for grinding the
_“saved ’’ constables of the D.M.P. is held, we hear, in
Merrion Hall. A strange thing is that according to
our reliable information it has never been known that a
“saved ’’ candidate failed in the drill and duty test.
: Recently “saved '’ constables who had been passed over
I on the promotion list long ago have been promoted. It
appears there is a standing order made in the time when
that hardy true-blue, Sir D. Harrell, was Chief Com-
missioner to the effect that, no matter whether the
“saved ’’ qualified or not, that a certain number of them
should be promoted.

Some time ago the Prisons’ Board authorities applied
to the Police Commissioner for an experienced man of
the G. Division, 27 years of age, to fill a post in con-

-nection with the Detection of Criminals by means of
finger prints. The Police Commissioner was away at
the time and the assistant was in charge. However, it
happened the Prisons’ Board authorities were induced
to alter the age from 27 to 30, and a ““ Saved ’’ police-
man, who was not, and never was, in the G. Division,
was appointedto the fat job. This was rather hard on
the men of the G. Division, but then many of them
have the economic disadvantage of not being “Saved.”’

‘When the last vacancy occurred in the G Division,
members of the force were asked to send in their appli-
~ cations. A great number did so, but, without having
.appeared before the Commissioner, all applicants
~with the exception of one, were, we hear, told to go
back to their barracks. The one who was pitched into
the vacancy was “Saved.” “Idolatrous’’ constables
are very small fry in the D.M.P. The strength of the
- College Street Sub-Division of the B. Division is 140
men. Of these 14 are “Saved’; and of these 14
“Saved ’’ 12 are specials; the other two are recruits.
The “ Baved ’’ in the B. Division are evidently no small
-y, Of these specials four have less than six years’
ce. :

At the meceting of the Waterford County Council in
Dungarvan, held on April 17th, the following resolution, e
proposed by Mr. James Hayes, was unanimously passed :
—“That, as it is now hopeless to expect a satisfactory
solution of the Irish University Question from either
English Party by the lukewarm methods hitherto em-
ployed, and as an immediate settlement is absolutely
necessary both in the interests of religion and National
progress, we ave convinced the time has come for the
Irvish people and the Irish Party to initiate a policy of
action vigorous, determined and aggressive. That we
respectfully beg to ask his Lordship the Bishop of Water-
ford to consider the advisability of helding in Dungarvan
at an early date a county convention, consisting of his
Lordship, the clergy, members of local elective bodies,
and representatives from each parish to deal with the
question.”

We feel sure that his Lordship of Waterford will be
only too pleased to lend a hand for this purpose. The
enemies of higher education have their Orange Drum ;
and it has been and is our view that the majority of this
country should make some kind of drum for their side.
That is why we have fought so hard for an organisation
of some kind to defend the Temporal rights and interests
of the majority of the citizens of this country. Ethics
have been worked for all they are worth in favour of a
rational settlement of the University grievance. Even
Mx. Balfour is convinced. But the country sees now that:
cthics are despised and useless in the matter. The
Dynamics of the Orangemen and others rule the situa- 3
tion.  The moral for the outraged majority is plain.
Ethics are obsolete weapons in this encounter; they had
better look now to Dynamics.

object of this meeting should commend itsolf

7T HIS week we print another most interesting and
vigorous batch of letters from well-known Irish
manufacturers on our industrial policy in so far as it
concerns the relations of the consumers towards Irish
goods. Last week our readers will recollect Messrs. S.
Irwin and Son, Paper Manufacturers; the firm of Rath-
borne dating back to 1488, Candle Manufacturers;
Messrs. M. Mahony Bros., Ltd., Blarney, Cloth Manu-
facturers; Messrs. James Watson and Co., Stained
Glass Makers and Church Decorators of Youghal; Mr.
J. A. O’Connell, Art Marble Works, Cork ; The City of
Galway Woollen Manufacturing Co., Ltd., Galway, and
the Lee Boot Manufacturing Co., Cork, stated their
views, and one and all, of course, agreed with the views
so frequently and forcibly set forth in the LEADER.
This week we print the views of the great Wexford
Agricultural Implement and Bicycle Firm, Messrs.
Philip Pierce and Co.; of Messrs. W. Dennehy and
Sons, the enterprising and expanding Hat Manufactur-
ing Firm; Messrs. P. J. Carroll and Co., Tobacco
Manufacturers, of Dundalk; Messrs. John McDonnel
and Co., Ltd., the Paper Makers of Saggart, Co.
Dublin; Messrs. E. Ryan and Co., Ltd., Soap Manu-
facturers, Cork ; Messrs. James O’Mara and Son, Bacon -
TFactory, Limerick, and Messrs. F. Smyth and Son,
Umbrella Manufacturers, 75 Grafton Street, Dublin.
Our readers will no doubt be struck by the sturdy
and independent tone of all the letters. Messrs. Philip
Pierce and Co., who have found a market for their
machinery and bicycles in face of the world’s-competi-
tion, ought to know what they are talking about; how-
ever, we suppose the few sentimentalists who, with the
best intentions in the world, no doubt, have been doing
great injury to Irish manufacture by their foolish talk
about spoon-feeding Irish manufacturers at the expense
of purchasers—we suppose these sentimentalists know
a great deal more about the matter than Philip Pierce
and Co., of Wexford! It will be noticed that Messrs.
W. Dennehy and Sons, of Cork, expressly state “ Now,
we think, that Irish manufacturers should protest
against this view, which, in our experience, is doing a
great deal of harm.”” Messrs. James O’Mara and Son,
of Limerick, remark that they sell 75 per cent. of
their products in the English, Continental, and Colonial
markets. That fact, as they point out, indicates a
state of apathy in the support of Irish goods in the
home market. It is obvious to any shrewd man that
this absurd sentimental cry of giving more for things
because they are Irish, plays into the enemies’ hands,
and helps to defeat the purpose of a business-like indus-
trial revival by creating an impression that the support -
of Irish goods is to be accompanied by a financial sacri-
fice on the part of the purchaser. It would be to the
interest of British manufacturers who are being hit
the Irish market by the increasing support given to
Trish goods that this absurd and thoughtless proposition
should be popularised.  Next week we will publish *
another batch of manufacturers’ opinions. X

CO-EDUCATION IN IRELAND.

“Most Rev, Dr, Foley in a notable letter defined the
issue at stake as ¢ Whether 1,000 separate schools are to
disappear within the next few years and to be replaced
by 500 others in which boys and girls of all ages from
3 to 18 are to be educated together. Is amalgamation
to be or not to be? That is the question!’ ’—7'he Irish
Independent, April 14, 1905.

These words of Dr. Foley make known to the Irish
people the real issue at stake in the battle against “the
New Rule.”” There has been a good deal said about the
hardship that would be done to the teachers, should this
rule be put into effect. Scarcely any proposal can be
made for the public good nowadays that does not carry
with it some damage to individual interests. The rail-
ways, tram-cars, electric lighting were advantages that
all entailed hardships on large sections of the community.
The closing of the police barracks and the operation of
the Land Act—both very beneficial to the community—
will throw a large number of persons out of employment.
Yet, if a scheme is manifestly for the public good, the
individual interest must be sacrificed. Salus populs
suprema lex. In the present circumstances, if the amal-
gamation of the schools were a benefit, any fairminded
government should compensate men who were thus un-
expectedly and undeservedly thrown out of a career they
had chosen, relying upon the stability of affairs, and
popular opinion should see to the making of this com-
pensation.

What most concerns the managers and the people of
Ireland is that this rule is substantially an attempt to
introduce co-education into this country.  The eager-
ness with which manual instruction was taken up by the
authorities of Marlborough Street shows that the Ameri-
can system of education has a strong pull on them. That
line of teaching was practically originated in the States
and was “boomed,”’ to use an Americanism, for all it
was worth. Co-education, that is, the mingling of the
sexes in the school-room, is also an American idea. The
movement began in Ohio in 1833, and since then has
spread with great rapidity. At present, in the State
elementary education the general practice is to have
boys and girls taught in the same classes. In fact,
wherever the opposite is found there are special condi-
tions such as location or structure of school building that
prevent its adoption. Of a total of 6,005 State high
schools, 98 per cent. are mixed, and even of private
schools there are 56 per cent. worked on the same plan.
In higher institutions, such as universities, technical
colleges, etc., there are 80 per cent at least in which the
soxes are educated together. In all these instances, par-
ticularly as regards elementary and high schools, the Pro-
testant spirit is the ruling one. In Catholic schools the
separation of the sexes is maintained. “When, therefore,
we read educational reports in which thereis great praise
given to the success of this system, and when the writers
stato that on the score of morality there can be nothin,
said in dispraise of it, we should bear in mind that these
are non-Catholics writing of their own co-religionists.
The standard is very different on this point between
Catholic and Protestant. Matters which in our theology
would be considered as of grave moment either per sz or
on account of consequences do not come within the scope

THE GREAT WHITE PLAGUE.

NLY the dweller in a Dublin slum could have written
O the haunting lines of James Clarence Mapguy —

«Ho also folt for all souls that suffer

Here, and in hell.”
There are slums cqually dirty and equally drem-y' in Gla's-
gow and in London, but somehow there is a sottishness in
the misery of their inhabitants which acts as a blessed ano-
dyno and keeps them dead to any but the merely animal
instinots. The spirituality of the Celt is his greatest bless-
ing and his groatest curse; it is no imaginary attribute, but
real enough to malke him feel, at least in some vague sense,
that abomination of desolation in which he is immersed in
Dublin’s slums. And that sweet sympathy to feel another’s
woe, which the Celt so seldom lacks, made poor Mangan weep
for those in the next stage of misery—in hell.

There is a weird and entrancing beauty in that drawing
of Clarence Mangan, done after his death, in the National
Gallery. It reminds you of the beautiful face in marble of
Shelley, at Magdalen College, but Shelley’s face has but the
imaginary perfection of a young Greek god, whilst Mangan’s,
scarcely less sublime, is a real portrait of a man who wove
thoughts that breathe and words that burn in the Dublin
slums—who flew to ardent spirits for solace as De Quincey did
to opium and Goldsmith to antimony. But to me it is also
the face of a man cut off in his prime by consumption, not by
debauch.

There is another marvellously realistic picture near at
hand of a consumptive—one of the Sheridans, T believe,
leaving for an ocean voyage—the panacea for phthisis of our
fathers. He is seated on deck, smothered with rugs, with
anxious friends behind. His long fingers, his thin hands,
his lank hair, his long-drawn, languorous face, his lustrous
eyes, his dry and swollen lips, are so vividly life-like that as
you gaze on the picture you seem to hear his panting breath,
his gasping cough, and his querulous complaint. Still, sur-
rounded as he is by luxury, his intellectual face is merely
that of a consumptive, doomed to die, yet wholly wrapped
up in himself with the characteristic egotism of the victim
of this fell disease, whose only thought is for his ease. Poor
Mangan's face is that of a consumptive, too. The witless,
heartless wight who suggested that he died of drink must

- Nothing in history, and nothing in literature, is so

Ugolino, whose three children were confined to a tower at

day -they implored for help from their agonising father.
Their sufferings ended within &' week, yet the world has not
ceased to sorrow for them. Till the end of time the story
will be told of the excruciating anguish of the night in the
Black Hole at Calcutta, yet the tragedy terminated in a few
hours. But the long-drawn agony of the Dublin consump-
tive's brief career is so common that we think it common-
place; we see it so incessantly that wo think it sordid rather
than sad.

The story is always the same. Patrick Byrne, a smart
young labourer, with a young wife and six children, lives
in a room in the Coombe. " He gets a wetting one day at his
work and remains in his damp clothes, for he has no others.
He gets a chill and develops a cough. The anxious wife
gives him the remedy which all the neighbours insist on, rum
and gruel, and insists ‘’twill be all right.” Patrick stays
in bed for a couple of days and gets anxious lest he lose his
job. He persuades himself that he is better and starts work
again. He struggles on for a couple of months, getting
thinner cvery day and coughing half the night. Still the
wife persuades herself that ‘“’tis only a stomach cough, and
will be better when the weather gets settled, with the help of
God.” Another wetting and the poor man gets still worse,
again he stays a couple of days in bed, and again he tries to
go to his work. But this time he is seized with such a cough
on getting up that he spits blood. Now both himself and
his wife are thoroughly alarmed and send for the dispensary
doctor and the priest. The latter tells him not to set his
heart on this world and the former speaks of the chance he
might have if he could get fresh air and good food. That is
the beginning of the end. The cough gets worse, and the
night sweats set in; the blood spitting continues and the
patient wastes away. Meanwhile almost every article in the
poor room is pawned. To keep the poor man warm both door
and windows are closed, and wife and children breathe the
air reeking with germs of disease. Towards the end he is
sent to the workhouse infirmary, there he gets wine and*
nourishment such as might have saved him in his earlier
stages, but all too late. When he dies the wife struggles
against fate for a few months. She tries to do a few days’
washing and her mother minds the children. But during
these few months the children have become so impregnated

and good food, you can cure many of them. The Corpora-

the English House of Commons, to put down “smug-
gling, and divers other modes of defrauding the
Revenue by illicit use of the tobacco plant,” it was
Richard Brinsley Sheridan who, speaking up for his
own country, wittily said, “ The Bill was a heap of
contradictions and absurdities thrown together by some
person who could write but could not read,” and the
witty M.P. went on to say— The formation of Bills
of Revenue was much like the process of the manu-
facture of tobacco itself. The Bills were presented in
the stalk, or short cut, to the Boards by some of the
chief members; there they were dried, and thence sent
to the Treasury mill to be ground and sifted; they
received a little infusion in a preliminary and recom-
mendatory speech from one of the great law officers of
the Crown, and then an application was made to the
House of Commons as merely for a permit to pass into
law. Such was the usual process, from which who
could expect a complete and perfect manufactured
article.”” But, as the Journals of the House of Com-
mons for the year 1790 record—Sheridan was beaten by
a majority of 44, Pitt’s Bill being passed by 191 for

The principal of an Engineering and Machine Making
Firm of London, writing to a cousin of his in Dublin un-
der date 17th April, says :—I have before me a somewhat
peculiar enquiry from Central Africa. As you are well
aware, there is a great deal of wool spun by hand i
Ireland, and the spinners use a spinning wheel’ as it
is called, worked by hand or by foot; the enquiry I
have received is for 50 such spinning wheels. A firm
wants to import them, and to set the natives to spin
wool, goats’ hair and cotton. I have no doubt that
there are in Ireland two or three makers of these spin-
ning wheels, and I shall feel exceedingly obliged if you
would endeavour to find out the names of the makers,
so as to enable me to place an order for 50 of the
machines. I expect they are made by joiners or similar
people in a small way, still, it is quite possible that
one man may have distinguished himself above others
in making good wheels, and might have a reputation
for them.”” The recipient of this letter called to the
Leaper Office.  We trust that the extract we have
given will meet the eyes of those capable of executing
this order for Central Africa. We shall be glad to
forward any replies to the proper quarter.
